era_context: The Neo-Assyrian Empire under Shalmaneser V remains at the height of its power, with firm control over the Levant and Mesopotamia. The Greek Archaic period continues with ongoing cultural development and colonisation. In China, the Spring and Autumn period proceeds with regional state competition. Egypt is fragmented under competing Libyan and Nubian dynasties.

events.title: Shalmaneser V's continued dominance and Assyrian imperial administration
events.description: Shalmaneser V's reign continues to define the Neo-Assyrian Empire's structure and reach. The Assyrian vassal state system, in which regional powers acknowledge Assyrian overlordship and pay tribute, remains firmly in place. The Assyrian administrative system, based on provincial organisation and the deportation of conquered populations, ensures control over vast territories. This period represents the consolidation of Assyrian hegemony that will persist until the empire's fall in 609 BCE.

sources.name: Assyrian Royal Annals (cuneiform inscriptions from Nineveh and Calah)

events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser V's reign and Assyrian dominance are well-attested in both Assyrian and biblical sources. The general political situation is clearly established.

events.title: Greek colonisation and the establishment of trade networks in the Mediterranean
events.description: Greek colonisation of the western Mediterranean continues, with settlements in southern Italy and Sicily becoming established centres of trade and culture. These colonies facilitate the exchange of goods, ideas, and technologies between the Greek world and the western Mediterranean. The establishment of trade networks during this period will have long-term consequences for Mediterranean commerce and cultural exchange.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (Book VI)
Archaeological evidence from colonial sites (pottery, settlement patterns)

events.certainty_note: The timing and nature of Greek colonisation are inferred from archaeological evidence and later literary sources. Precise dating of individual settlements is uncertain.

events.title: Spring and Autumn period: regional state consolidation in China
events.description: The Spring and Autumn period continues with regional states consolidating power and competing for hegemony. The Zhou Dynasty's nominal authority persists, but real power rests with regional rulers. This period is characterised by diplomatic alliances, military campaigns, and the gradual emergence of a system of competing states that will eventually lead to the Warring States period (475–221 BCE).

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn period is well-documented in Chinese sources. The general political situation and trajectory are clearly established.

events.title: Egyptian fragmentation under the 22nd–23rd dynasties
events.description: Egypt during the Late Period (664–332 BCE) is fragmented under competing Libyan and Nubian dynasties. The 22nd Dynasty (c. 945–715 BCE) and 23rd Dynasty (c. 828–712 BCE) rule simultaneously in different regions, with the 22nd Dynasty controlling Lower Egypt and the 23rd Dynasty controlling Upper Egypt. This fragmentation reflects the decline of central authority and the rise of regional power centres, particularly among Libyan military families who have settled in Egypt.
key_figures: Shoshenq III (22nd Dynasty, r. c. 825–773 BCE)
Osorkon III (23rd Dynasty, r. c. 787–759 BCE)

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and temple inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the Late Period is notoriously uncertain. Regnal dates for 22nd and 23rd Dynasty rulers vary significantly among scholars. The dating of individual rulers and the precise extent of their authority are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: Egyptian chronology for the 22nd–23rd dynasties is highly disputed. Different scholarly systems place these dynasties at different dates, with variations of ±20–30 years not uncommon. The extent of overlap between the 22nd and 23rd dynasties and the precise territorial divisions between them are debated. Additionally, the traditional dating of Greek colonisation events remains uncertain and is not supported by contemporary evidence.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 760 BCE is moderate overall but highly regionally uneven. Assyrian sources are abundant and contemporary but propagandistic. Greek sources are sparse and much later, introducing significant uncertainty. Chinese sources are reliable for general political trends but lack year-by-year precision. Egyptian sources are fragmentary and chronologically uncertain. The record is heavily weighted toward the Near East and Mediterranean; sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, the Americas, and Southeast Asia are essentially absent from contemporary documentation. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate, centralised societies; the histories of non-literate or decentralised societies are largely invisible in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser V's campaigns against Israel culminate in the siege and fall of Samaria in 722 BCE
Greek colonisation and Chinese state consolidation occur in the same era, representing parallel processes of political and economic reorganisation
